description: Strict contract for deep_research mode outputs. The result must be a structured research artifact that links claims to evidence, preserves uncertainty explicitly when needed, and ends with a synthesis suitable for long-form delivery. The artifact must not be a loose note dump, unsupported summary, or citation-light essay.

requirements: The artifact must identify a clear research topic, question, or scope.
The scope must be specific enough that findings and synthesis can be evaluated against it.
The topic definition must not be empty, generic, or placeholder text.
The artifact must include exactly one executive summary.
The executive summary must summarize the most decision-relevant conclusions, not merely restate the topic.
The executive summary must remain concise relative to the full artifact while still communicating the central answer, major findings, and material caveats.
The executive summary must not introduce claims that are absent from the findings or unsupported by cited evidence.
The artifact must contain an explicit ordered findings section or findings list.
Each finding must state a clear claim, supporting evidence, and associated citation references.
Each finding must distinguish observed evidence from interpretation when both appear.
Each finding must be understandable without requiring the reader to infer missing support.
The artifact must end with a final synthesis suitable for long-form delivery.
The synthesis must integrate the findings into a coherent answer, explanation, or narrative rather than repeat them mechanically.
The synthesis must preserve material caveats, tradeoffs, and uncertainty rather than smoothing them away.
The synthesis must be usable as downstream long-form source material without major researcher intervention.
The artifact must include an explicit source list or source objects.
Each cited source must be identifiable through stable citation metadata such as title, authoring entity, publication name, URL, date, or equivalent source handle.
Sources must be sufficient for a downstream reader or system to trace evidence back to origin.

rules: The executive summary must appear before detailed findings.
The executive summary must communicate the main answer or thesis in direct language.
The executive summary must cover the most important findings only and avoid low-signal detail overload.
The executive summary must mention the most material uncertainty, limitation, or evidence gap when such uncertainty affects interpretation.
Any factual or analytical claim in the executive summary must be traceable to at least one finding and its supporting citation set.
The executive summary must not contain drafting notes, methodological chatter, or unsupported speculation.
Every finding must contain at least one explicit claim.
Every material claim must be linked to supporting evidence and one or more citations.
Evidence references must be specific enough to show why the cited source supports the claim.
If a finding synthesizes multiple sources, the finding must cite each materially supporting source rather than only one representative source.
If a claim is an inference rather than a directly stated source fact, the finding must make that inferential step explicit.
If sources materially disagree, the finding must describe the disagreement instead of collapsing it into a single overstated claim.
Findings must not present conjecture, common knowledge assumptions, or background filler as evidence-backed conclusions.
The artifact must preserve uncertainty whenever the evidence is incomplete, conflicting, indirect, time-sensitive, or otherwise insufficient for high-confidence conclusion.
Uncertainty must be attached to the relevant claim, finding, or synthesis statement rather than hidden in a generic disclaimer.
The artifact must distinguish between verified facts, plausible inferences, contested claims, and unresolved questions.
When evidence conflicts, the output must describe the conflict and, when possible, explain which interpretation is better supported and why.
When important information is missing or unverified, the output must say so explicitly instead of implying certainty.
Confidence language, if used, must match the evidence strength and must not overstate what the sources establish.
Time-sensitive claims should preserve recency context or as-of timing when that timing materially affects reliability.
Every material factual claim must have citation coverage.
Every analytical or comparative claim must cite the evidence base that materially supports the analysis.
Each finding must include citation references directly alongside or immediately attached to the supported claim or evidence summary.
The final synthesis must cite or clearly inherit support from the cited findings for all non-trivial conclusions.
High-stakes, surprising, contested, or quantitative claims require especially clear citation coverage and should not rely on a single weak reference when stronger corroboration is needed.
Citations must resolve to entries in the source list without ambiguity.
A source list alone is insufficient; citation linkage inside the research body is required.
The final synthesis must integrate findings into a coherent, long-form-ready narrative or answer.
The synthesis must prioritize the strongest supported conclusions and avoid giving equal weight to minor or weakly supported points.
The synthesis must reconcile cross-source evidence where possible and make unresolved tensions explicit where reconciliation is not possible.
The synthesis must separate what is well supported from what remains uncertain or conditional.
The synthesis must add interpretive value beyond a list of findings while remaining faithful to the cited evidence.
The synthesis must not introduce new unsupported claims, recommendations, or factual assertions.
The overall artifact must read as a polished research deliverable rather than a collection of excerpts, bookmarks, or browsing notes.
The artifact must present a clear progression from topic framing to executive summary to evidence-linked findings to final synthesis.
Section ordering must support comprehension and traceability.
Headings, finding identifiers, or structured fields must make it possible to reference specific claims and their supporting evidence deterministically.
Background context is allowed only when it materially supports the research question or interpretation of findings.
The artifact must end cleanly without prompt scaffolding, notes to self, or generation metadata.

validation_rules: output_type must be one of the supported_output_types.
The artifact must include exactly one executive summary.
At least one finding must exist.
Every finding must include a non-empty claim and non-empty supporting evidence description.
Every material claim in the executive summary, findings, and final synthesis must have citation coverage.
All citations must resolve unambiguously to declared sources.

The artifact must satisfy executive_summary_requirements, evidence_linked_findings_requirements, uncertainty_handling_rules, citation_coverage_requirements, synthesis_quality_rules, and research_structure_expectations.
The artifact must preserve uncertainty explicitly where evidence does not justify certainty.
The final synthesis must be suitable for long-form delivery rather than only bullet-point recap.
No explanatory meta-commentary may appear inside the artifact payload.
If JSON output is requested by the caller, the surrounding response must remain valid JSON only.
failure_conditions: Missing executive summary, findings section, final synthesis, or source list.
Claims appear without linked evidence or without resolvable citations.
The executive summary contains unsupported conclusions or introduces claims absent from the body.
Findings are generic summaries, loose notes, or quotations without explicit claim-and-evidence structure.
Uncertainty, evidence gaps, or source conflicts are omitted, buried, or falsely resolved.
The final synthesis is merely a repetition of findings and does not integrate them into a coherent long-form answer.
Citations exist only as a bibliography with no inline or per-finding linkage.
Sources are too vague to identify, trace, or audit.
The artifact overstates confidence relative to the evidence base.
The output reads as brainstorming, browsing notes, or raw source excerpts instead of a structured research deliverable.
Schema fields are omitted, renamed, or supplemented in ways that break the contract.
Extra commentary appears outside the declared artifact structure.
